meta.note: 80-150 word answers in Jamie's voice, adapted from voice corpus. Draft for review before submission.

answers.question: Why are you interested in this role?
answers.answer: I'm drawn to this role because it sits right at the intersection of two things I care about: running programs end-to-end and improving quality through data. In my current role I manage 20+ programs and coordinate 10+ vendors—gathering requirements, defining success criteria, and refining delivery through feedback loops. The Human Data role is that same work, applied to something I find genuinely exciting: the data that shapes how a model behaves. I don't just want to execute a program—I want to ask the right questions, find where quality breaks down, and build processes that make trainers' work better and faster. That diagnose-design-measure rhythm is exactly how I like to work.

answers.question: Why OpenAI?
answers.answer: I'll be candid—what pulls me to OpenAI is personal. I've been building a RAG-based system with a tech friend that evaluates job fit from my own resumes and experience, and it taught me firsthand how much the quality of the underlying data determines what a model can actually do. Ultimately, I want to work at OpenAI because I'm passionate about building human expertise alongside AI technology. I treat organizational challenges as systems to be studied, relying on data, internal insights, and stakeholder partnership to design solutions that scale. OpenAI is one of the few places where that mindset meets work that genuinely matters at the frontier, and I'd be honored to contribute to it.

answers.question: Describe your experience managing programs/vendors end-to-end.
answers.answer: As a Program Manager at InGenius Prep, I own 20+ programs and coordinate 10+ external vendors plus cross-functional teams from start to finish. I begin with needs assessments across 25+ global stakeholders to gather requirements and surface the real gaps, then define success criteria—measurable outcomes, not just activity. I write the guides and workflows that keep delivery consistent (that documentation reduced onboarding time by 75%), facilitate training for 600+ participants, and use feedback analysis to continuously refine positioning and operations. One lesson that stuck: I once launched a process assuming adoption would follow—it didn't. I went back, interviewed the team, found the real issue was friction, and rebuilt it. Assumption is the most expensive thing in program work, so I build in feedback loops early.
